description: Microsoft profiles: tenant-owned named Microsoft profiles exist as the shared credential model for Teams.
Microsoft profiles: profile persistence stores tenant-scoped profile ID, display name, client ID, client secret reference, tenant ID, and readiness metadata.
Microsoft profiles: profile names are unique within a tenant.
Microsoft profiles: one profile can be marked as the tenant default for legacy Microsoft consumers.
Microsoft profiles: migrating from the singleton Microsoft configuration creates a default named profile automatically.
Microsoft profiles: migration preserves the current `microsoft_client_id` value for the default profile.
Microsoft profiles: migration preserves the current `microsoft_client_secret` value for the default profile.
Microsoft profiles: migration preserves the current `microsoft_tenant_id` value for the default profile.
Microsoft profiles: profile reads return masked secret state instead of raw secret material.
Microsoft profiles: profile readiness captures whether required credentials exist and whether consumer-specific setup can proceed.
Microsoft profiles: profile records can be archived without destroying historical consumer bindings.
Microsoft profiles: profile listing returns only the current tenant's profiles.
Microsoft profiles: profile creation validates required display name, client ID, client secret, and tenant ID fields.
Microsoft profiles: profile update supports rotating the client secret without forcing the UI to display the previous secret.
Microsoft profiles: deleting or archiving a profile that is still actively bound to a consumer is blocked until the binding is changed.
Microsoft profiles: the secret provider remains the storage system of record for profile credential material.
Microsoft profiles: profile reads and writes remain fully tenant-scoped.
Microsoft profiles: helper resolution returns the tenant default profile for compatibility paths when a consumer has no explicit binding.
Microsoft settings UI: the existing singleton Microsoft settings screen becomes a profile manager.
Microsoft settings UI: the profile manager lists all profiles with display name, tenant ID, default state, and readiness state.
Microsoft settings UI: admins can create a new named Microsoft profile from the settings screen.
Microsoft settings UI: admins can edit an existing named Microsoft profile from the settings screen.
Microsoft settings UI: admins can archive an existing named Microsoft profile from the settings screen.
Microsoft settings UI: admins can mark a named Microsoft profile as the default profile.
Microsoft settings UI: the form captures display name, client ID, client secret, and tenant ID.
Microsoft settings UI: the form preserves the existing client secret when the admin edits metadata without rotating the secret.
Microsoft settings UI: the screen continues to show the Microsoft Entra outbound link.
Microsoft settings UI: the screen shows Teams-required redirect URIs for each profile.
Microsoft settings UI: the screen shows existing email, calendar, and MSP SSO redirect URIs for each profile.
Microsoft settings UI: the screen shows Teams-required scope guidance for each profile.
Microsoft settings UI: the screen shows which consumers are currently bound to each profile.
Microsoft settings UI: the screen surfaces readiness or validation errors inline per profile.
Microsoft settings UI: the screen has an explicit empty state when no Microsoft profiles exist.
Microsoft settings UI: the screen retains a refresh path for reloading readiness and masked secret state.
Microsoft settings UI: destructive archive or delete actions require confirmation.
Microsoft settings UI: the Teams setup surface can be reached directly from profile management.
Consumer bindings: a tenant-scoped consumer binding model exists for Microsoft-profile consumers.
Consumer bindings: the model supports at least MSP SSO, email, calendar, and Teams as consumer types.
Consumer bindings: each consumer can reference exactly one selected Microsoft profile per tenant.
Consumer bindings: migration creates compatibility bindings so existing Microsoft consumers continue to work after profile introduction.
Consumer bindings: Teams requires an explicit selected profile instead of silently falling back to unrelated global Microsoft credentials.
Teams setup: a tenant-scoped Teams integration record exists.
Teams setup: the Teams integration record stores the selected Microsoft profile ID.
Teams setup: the Teams integration record stores install/configuration status for the tenant.
Teams setup: the Teams integration record stores enabled Teams capabilities for the tenant.
Teams setup: the Teams integration record stores personal-notification category preferences for the tenant.
Teams setup: the Teams integration record stores allowed action configuration for Teams quick actions.
Teams setup: a tenant-admin Teams setup UI exists.
Teams setup: the UI requires selecting one Microsoft profile before Teams can be activated.
Teams setup: the UI shows the selected profile's app-registration values, redirect URIs, and scope guidance.
Teams setup: the UI shows install / consent / readiness checklist state for Teams.
Teams setup: the UI can save draft setup progress before Teams is fully activated.
Teams setup: the UI can activate Teams once required setup is complete.
Teams setup: the UI can deactivate Teams without deleting the underlying Microsoft profile.
Teams setup: the UI shows the currently selected profile, current capability toggles, and notification categories.
Teams setup: the UI links back to Microsoft profile management when no eligible profile exists or the selected profile is invalid.
Teams setup: the settings navigation includes a Teams integration entry.
Teams setup: a tenant without an eligible Microsoft profile receives a guided setup state instead of a blank error.
Teams app package: a Teams app manifest/package model exists for PSA.
Teams app package: the manifest declares a personal tab.
Teams app package: the manifest declares a personal-scope bot.
Teams app package: the manifest declares a message extension.
Teams app package: the manifest declares activity-feed notification capability.
Teams app package: the manifest uses the selected Microsoft profile's application identity and `webApplicationInfo` metadata.
Teams app package: the manifest supports personal scope for the tab and bot.
Teams app package: the manifest supports message and compose contexts needed for message-extension flows.
Teams app package: the manifest omits channel-routing-specific notification requirements in v1.
Teams app package: tenant install state supports at least not-configured, install-pending, active, and error states.
Teams app package: setup surfaces can retrieve the tenant's current package/install metadata.
Teams app package: bot IDs, app IDs, and package metadata are stored with the Teams integration record as needed.
Teams app package: environment-specific base URLs are generated correctly for local, staging, and production packaging flows.
Teams app package: changing the selected Teams profile invalidates stale package/install readiness state.
Teams app package: setup surfaces can present a package download or install handoff for the tenant.
Teams app package: deep-link targets used by notifications and action results stay consistent with the manifest capabilities.
Teams identity: Teams authentication uses the tenant-selected Microsoft profile instead of an unrelated global Microsoft credential path.
Teams identity: tab SSO resolves PSA tenant and MSP user context.
Teams identity: bot SSO resolves PSA tenant and MSP user context.
Teams identity: message extension SSO resolves PSA tenant and MSP user context.
Teams identity: only MSP users are accepted into Teams flows in v1.
Teams identity: client-portal users are rejected from Teams flows in v1.
Teams identity: Teams-authenticated requests reuse existing NextAuth/MSP SSO user resolution patterns where possible.
Teams identity: missing, inactive, or unready selected profiles block Teams auth with admin-readable remediation state.
Teams identity: user mapping can associate a Teams user identity to the correct PSA user within the tenant.
Teams identity: requests from the wrong Microsoft / Teams tenant are rejected for the PSA tenant.
Teams identity: deep-link entry points can bootstrap the destination without a separate PSA sign-in prompt inside Teams.
Teams identity: expired or invalid sessions return a Teams-safe reauthentication path instead of raw OAuth errors.
Teams identity: tenant resolution works correctly for Teams entry points across multiple vanity hosts or tenant slugs.
Teams identity: rebinding Teams to a new Microsoft profile invalidates stale auth assumptions for future requests.
Teams identity: authorization checks still run after Teams authentication before entity access is granted.
Teams identity: the selected profile takes precedence over broad app/global Microsoft env credentials for Teams-specific flows.
Teams identity: Teams surface handlers can distinguish unauthenticated, unauthorized, and not-configured tenant states.
Teams identity: Teams-facing auth errors remain safe to show inside Teams UI constraints.
Personal tab: a Teams personal tab entry point exists for PSA.
Personal tab: the default landing view opens a PSA “my work” style starting point for technicians.
Personal tab: deep links can open a specific ticket view.
Personal tab: deep links can open a specific project task view.
Personal tab: deep links can open a specific approval view.
Personal tab: deep links can open a specific time-entry-related workflow view.
Personal tab: deep links can open related contact or client context when invoked from Teams message workflows.
Personal tab: the initial tab load shows enough record context to confirm the user landed on the intended PSA entity.
Personal tab: inaccessible or deleted entities fall back to a safe PSA landing state with an explanatory message.
Personal tab: the tab offers an “open in full PSA” path when the user needs richer context or unsupported functionality.
Personal tab: the Teams tab preserves PSA authorization semantics for every rendered entity or action.
Personal tab: the tab prefers reusing existing PSA screens/components instead of building parallel Teams-only record UIs.
Personal tab: Teams bootstrap passes user and tenant context into existing PSA UI composition safely.
Personal tab: activity-feed notification deep links open the correct tab destination.
Personal tab: bot action results can open the correct tab destination.
Personal tab: message extension results can open the correct tab destination.
Personal tab: the landing experience handles cold-start setup or not-configured tenants gracefully.
Personal tab: the tab remains the escalation path for workflows that are too complex for cards or dialogs.
Teams action layer: a shared Teams action registry exists for bot commands, message-extension actions, and card/dialog submits.
Teams action layer: each supported action declares its required inputs and target entity types.
Teams action layer: shared entity resolvers handle tickets, tasks, contacts, approvals, and time-entry targets.
Teams action layer: shared authorization checks wrap the underlying PSA services or server actions.
Teams action layer: shared result mappers return Teams-safe summaries, buttons, and deep links.
Teams action layer: validation errors use one consistent response shape across bot, message extension, and cards/dialogs.
Teams action layer: action execution records the invoking surface so downstream PSA behavior can distinguish bot, message extension, and tab contexts.
Teams action layer: deep-link generation reuses existing PSA deep-link helpers instead of building Teams-specific URL logic.
Teams action layer: actions can distinguish read-only lookups from mutating operations.
Teams action layer: actions reuse existing PSA ticket, task, approval, contact, and time-entry business operations where possible.
Teams action layer: actions can expose allowed-action metadata for a user and entity.
Teams action layer: Teams input payloads are normalized into PSA-ready requests before execution.
Teams action layer: duplicate card/dialog submissions can be handled safely without creating duplicate mutations.
Teams action layer: partial-failure results return user-readable remediation guidance.
Teams action layer: Teams capability gating can disable unsupported actions centrally.
Teams action layer: action definitions are reused instead of reimplementing each workflow per Teams surface.
Personal bot: a Teams personal-scope bot exists for PSA.
Personal bot: a command router parses supported bot commands.
Personal bot: unsupported commands return a help-oriented response instead of silently failing.
Personal bot: a welcome/help response lists the supported v1 commands.
Personal bot: bot responses can render buttons or cards that open the tab or quick actions.
Personal bot: the bot respects tenant-level allowed-action configuration.
Personal bot: explicit entity references in commands are resolved consistently.
Personal bot: unsupported non-personal contexts return a clear “personal scope only” response for v1.
Personal bot command `my tickets`: returns the technician's relevant tickets.
Personal bot command `my tickets`: response items include enough summary data to act or click through.
Personal bot command `my tickets`: response items include deep links into the Teams tab or PSA web app.
Personal bot command `ticket <id>`: returns a ticket summary for a valid ticket reference.
Personal bot command `ticket <id>`: returns a clear not-found or not-authorized response for invalid references.
Personal bot command `assign ticket`: supports assigning a ticket to a technician.
Personal bot command `assign ticket`: can resolve the target ticket from command input or current bot workflow context.
Personal bot command `assign ticket`: can resolve the target assignee from available PSA users.
Personal bot command `assign ticket`: returns the updated assignment summary after success.
Personal bot command `add note`: supports appending an internal note to a target ticket.
Personal bot command `add note`: can resolve the target ticket from explicit input or current workflow context.
Personal bot command `add note`: returns the saved note result and deep link after success.
Personal bot command `reply to contact`: supports adding a customer-facing reply for a target ticket/contact context.
Personal bot command `reply to contact`: can prefill or resolve the ticket/contact context before submit.
Personal bot command `reply to contact`: returns the reply result and follow-up deep link after success.
Personal bot command `log time`: supports creating a time entry against a ticket.
Personal bot command `log time`: supports creating a time entry against a project task.
Personal bot command `log time`: collects minimal duration, note, and work-context data.
Personal bot command approvals: returns the technician's pending approvals.
Personal bot command approvals: can execute approve/reject or request-change actions for supported approval items.
Message extension: a Teams message extension exists for PSA.
Message extension: search commands can look up PSA tickets.
Message extension: search commands can look up PSA tasks.
Message extension: search commands can look up PSA contacts.
Message extension: search commands can look up approval-related work items needed for v1.
Message extension: search commands work from compose and command-box contexts.
Message extension: action commands work from message context.
Message extension: search queries remain tenant-scoped.
Message extension: search queries respect the invoking user's PSA permissions.
Message extension: search results support pagination or continuation for longer result sets.
Message extension: search results show compact summary information appropriate to Teams.
Message extension: search results include deep links to open the selected record in the Teams tab or PSA web app.
Message extension: search results can surface allowed quick actions where supported.
Message extension: unsupported message types or contexts return a clear, recoverable response.
Message extension: the extension is unavailable when the tenant Teams integration is not active.
Message extension: the extension respects tenant-level allowed-action configuration.
Message extension: Teams-authenticated context is available to search and action commands.
Message extension: users can open a matching ticket directly from search results.
Message extension: users can open a matching task directly from search results.
Message extension: users can open a matching contact or approval directly from search results when permitted.
Message extension: command definitions remain focused on lookup and message-driven actions rather than general chat.
Message extension: result rendering reuses the shared Teams action/result model.
Message extension: command invocation records which surface triggered the action for downstream processing.
Message extension: search behavior prefers existing PSA data-access patterns rather than Teams-specific record indexes.
Message action `create ticket from message`: supports creating a new PSA ticket from a Teams message.
Message action `create ticket from message`: captures subject/summary/body data from the Teams message context.
Message action `create ticket from message`: supports minimal admin-approved ticket fields such as board, status, and client/contact context.
Message action `create ticket from message`: stores Teams source-message reference metadata with the created work item.
Message action `create ticket from message`: confirms success with a deep link to the created ticket.
Message action `create ticket from message`: guards against duplicate submit behavior.
Message action `update from message`: supports targeting an existing ticket.
Message action `update from message`: supports targeting an existing task when appropriate.
Message action `update from message`: supports appending an internal note derived from the Teams message.
Message action `update from message`: supports appending a customer-visible reply when the user has permission and the record state allows it.
Message action `update from message`: stores Teams source-message reference metadata with the update.
Message action `update from message`: confirms success with a deep link to the updated record.
Message action `update from message`: validates target-record permission before mutating PSA data.
Message action `update from message`: supports dialog handoff when more fields are needed than fit a one-click action.
Message action `update from message`: prefills action forms from the selected Teams message context.
Message action `create ticket from message`: can hand off to a richer tab flow when the selected message needs more complex ticket creation context.
Message action `update from message`: can hand off to the personal tab when the mutation is too complex for a dialog.
Message-driven actions: both create and update flows reuse the shared Teams action layer instead of bespoke handlers.
Quick actions: a shared adaptive-card/dialog submission path exists for Teams quick actions.
Quick actions: assign-ticket cards/dialogs collect the minimum required assignee and note inputs.
Quick actions: add-note cards/dialogs collect the minimum required note inputs.
Quick actions: reply-to-contact cards/dialogs collect the minimum required reply inputs.
Quick actions: log-time cards/dialogs collect the minimum required time-entry inputs.
Quick actions: approval cards/dialogs collect approval outcome and optional comment inputs.
Quick actions: forms can prefill fields from the invoking entity or Teams message context.
Quick actions: forms validate required fields before submission.
Quick actions: forms submit through the shared Teams action layer.
Quick actions: forms return success confirmations in Teams after submit.
Quick actions: forms return recoverable validation or state errors in Teams after submit.
Quick actions: forms support cancel/dismiss without side effects.
Quick actions: users only see quick actions they are allowed to perform.
Quick actions: the UI can redirect the user into the tab when the action exceeds quick-action complexity limits.
Teams notifications: a Teams delivery channel exists alongside existing PSA notification generation.
Teams notifications: tenant Teams setup stores category preferences for supported personal notifications.
Teams notifications: Teams delivery reuses existing notification payload generation rather than introducing a separate trigger catalog.
Teams notifications: Teams delivery reuses existing PSA deep-link resolution rather than building a second link system.
Teams notifications: recipient resolution can map an eligible PSA MSP user to a Teams user identity.
Teams notifications: delivery only targets users who have a valid Teams linkage/setup state.
Teams notifications: assignment-related events can trigger personal activity-feed notifications.
Teams notifications: customer-reply events can trigger personal activity-feed notifications.
Teams notifications: approval-request events can trigger personal activity-feed notifications.
Teams notifications: escalation events can trigger personal activity-feed notifications.
Teams notifications: SLA-risk events can trigger personal activity-feed notifications.
Teams notifications: payloads include enough title/body/entity context to identify the work item in Teams.
Teams notifications: payloads include deep links to the exact PSA record or workflow destination.
Teams notifications: category disablement at the tenant level suppresses Teams delivery for that category.
Teams notifications: missing user linkage suppresses Teams delivery without breaking the underlying PSA event.
Teams notifications: delivery state is captured in a reusable sent/delivered/failed model where available.
Teams notifications: clicking a notification opens the intended Teams tab or PSA web deep link destination.
Teams notifications: no queue-to-channel or board-to-channel mapping is required in v1.
Teams notifications: Teams delivery remains personal-notification-only in v1.
Teams notifications: the existing event bus/workflow infrastructure remains the notification trigger source.
Permissions: only authorized tenant admins can manage Microsoft profiles and Teams setup.
Permissions: tab, bot, message extension, and quick actions all enforce existing PSA authorization rules for the target entity/action.
Permissions: read-only users cannot execute mutating Teams actions.
Permissions: records outside the user's tenant or scope never appear in Teams search or lookup results.
Compatibility: migrating to named Microsoft profiles preserves current email, calendar, and MSP SSO behavior.
Compatibility: a default Microsoft profile binding exists immediately after migration.
Compatibility: rebinding Teams to a different Microsoft profile does not silently rebind unrelated Microsoft consumers.
Compatibility: archiving the currently selected Teams profile is blocked until Teams is rebound or deactivated.
Compatibility: deleting the currently selected Teams profile is blocked until Teams is rebound or deactivated.
Compatibility: switching the selected Teams profile invalidates stale Teams auth or install readiness state.
Permissions: client-portal user sign-in attempts are rejected explicitly for Teams v1.
Fallback: a tenant without Teams install/setup receives guided readiness messaging instead of raw errors in Teams surfaces.
Fallback: unsupported bot contexts return a clear personal-scope guidance response.
Fallback: unsupported commands return a help response rather than a dead end.
Fallback: selected profiles missing required Teams setup or consent show actionable admin remediation state.
Fallback: Teams actions are unavailable when the selected profile or Teams integration is inactive.
Fallback: both bot and message extension respect the tenant's allowed-action configuration.
Compatibility: legacy singleton Microsoft settings APIs keep a migration-safe compatibility path while profile-based consumers are introduced.
